cards.title: Meridian Beacon
cards.content: A Starfleet traffic-control platform marking the official western entrance to the Lacuna March.
cards.text: A Starfleet traffic-control platform marking the official western entrance to the Lacuna March.

cards.title: Meridian Beacon: Director
cards.content: The safest repair and resupply point and the most documented. Border Service, inquiry officers, customs archives, and Hadran liaison staff share a platform whose public map omits traffic its controllers privately manage. Everyday texture: Controllers maintain two route boards: the public display and a handwritten shift book of callsigns that do not officially exist. Families wait beside customs desks for destination rulings.

cards.title: Meridian Refugee Annex: Director
cards.content: The Annex can protect people briefly but cannot become a permanent destination. Its records are vulnerable to lawful requests that may still endanger applicants. Conditions here expose whether shipboard policy produces safety, delay, or unaccountable confinement. Everyday texture: Volunteers post meal times in four languages, children trade ship-recognition cards, and advocates keep paper copies because digital files can be frozen by competing jurisdictions.

cards.title: Halloway Colony

cards.text: A prosperous Federation agricultural colony whose exports depend on predictable routes and reliable inspection rules.

cards.title: Halloway Colony: Director
cards.content: Halloway leaders demand chart restoration, but cooperatives have hidden refugee laborers, provisioned Free Passage ships, and routed taxes from erased settlements. The colony can become a legitimate logistical ally or a political engine for closure. Everyday texture: Grain elevators share landing fields with family-run shuttles. Cooperative boards publish harvest schedules, while labor rosters are kept deliberately vague in districts that remember earlier purges.

cards.title: Gravesend Veil
cards.content: A sensor-distorting nebular fold used for concealed crossings between Meridian, Sable, and protected settlements.
cards.text: A sensor-distorting nebular fold used for concealed crossings between Meridian, Sable, and protected settlements.

cards.title: Gravesend Veil: Director
cards.content: Radiation pockets, old mines, reflected subspace returns, and changing shear make automated navigation unreliable. Local pilots preserve route knowledge through practice, oral teaching, and physical marker sequences. The Veil responds to probes, beacon work, and accumulated data; it is dangerous, not supernatural. Everyday texture: No permanent settlement exists, but pilots leave coded memorial tags on stable rocks and emergency caches in shielded hollows. Route knowledge is taught in pairs so no single arrest erases it.

Whether Sable develops accountable civic authority or competing checkpoints is a central campaign question. Everyday texture: Dock numbers are auctioned at dawn, disputes are heard in a converted cargo theater, and merchants accept three legal identities plus a local witness seal.

cards.title: Nysa's Lantern: Director
cards.content: The largest sanctuary in the March and the place most endangered by full chart publication. It has schools, workshops, elections, dissent, security problems, and residents who do not agree on whether recognition is protection or exposure. Everyday texture: Morning bells mark school shifts rather than religious observance. Hull sections are named for the ships that carried families to safety, and council sessions are broadcast internally unless a witness requests protection.

cards.content: Halden's last verified telemetry points here. Black Ledger uses the debris field for exchanges, but the system also supports unregistered homesteads and routine survival stories unrelated to the missing officer. Salvagers leave color-coded scrap markers that mean safety, claim, warning, or debt depending on who reads them.

cards.title: Far Lantern: Director
cards.content: Its archive contains unfiltered traffic history from before and during the chart erasures. Restoring it could expose refugee routes, Hadran deployments, Black Ledger traffic, and Starfleet complicity. Editing or destroying it can protect people while compromising accountability. Everyday texture: Keepers live in six-week rotations, maintain physical checksum books, and read unidentified distress bursts aloud before deleting duplicates.

canonEra.dominionWarStatus: The Dominion War has not begun. Do not use postwar reconstruction assumptions, Dominion occupation history, or later Starfleet wartime doctrine as current background.
canonEra.voyagerStatus: Voyager is missing in the Delta Quadrant and its fate is unknown to the Federation. It does not intersect this campaign.
canonEra.canonBoundary: Use canon factions, technology, uniforms, and early-2370s conditions as background. The ship, crew, Lacuna March, Hadran conflict, Protocol, and outcomes are original and canon-adjacent.

missionDirectionRules: Situation on rails, solution off rails.
The Lacuna March is a persistent region; locations, routes, factions, witnesses, and obligations continue outside the foreground quest.
Every dynamic event and front advance must have a causal parent in committed state, elapsed time, actor goals, or a physical process.
Travel advances stardate and may advance only causally ready fronts and clocks.
Model route physical status, confidence, visibility, access, and custodian as separable dimensions.
Keep protective redaction, privacy, non-recognition, coerced disappearance, archival loss, and criminal falsification distinct.
Calm side assignments and ordinary life may remain unrelated to Halden, the Protocol, Black Ledger, or Hadran operations.
Crew disagreement should be focused and professionally grounded; not every senior officer speaks in every decision.
No actor begins with complete campaign truth. Preserve knowledge boundaries and mistaken beliefs.
If the player bypasses an expected scene, move information only through a plausible alternate source with an actual cost.
Factions act between scenes according to goals, information, capacity, risk tolerance, and timing.
Draw the Border must read actual route state, allies, assets, evidence custody, faction posture, ship condition, Kellan and Halden status, crew readiness, and unresolved obligations.
hiddenInformation: Raw campaign track, clock, front, relationship, and loyalty values
Halden's exact location and full audit plan before established contact
The complete Far Lantern archive before recovery or justified access
The full participant map of the Lacuna Protocol
Kellan, Holt, Voss, Sorn, Renn, and local actors' hidden knowledge beyond revealed facts
Predetermined valid solutions, ending scores, or Command Decisions
failurePolicy: No single failed check ends a mission.
Campaign-critical investigation preserves alternate evidence routes through logs, witnesses, physical signatures, maintenance residue, route changes, archives, and actor behavior.
Failure may create delay, exposure, injury, route degradation, lost custody, public distrust, harder rescue, or transformed objectives.
Senior-crew death requires an established lethal situation, meaningful player choice or accumulated consequence, fair opportunity for command response, and narrative weight.
Refusing, postponing, delegating, withdrawing, or abandoning an assignment is a consequential command act, not an invalid input.
Loss of the Aster Vale, command relief, Halden, Far Lantern, a sanctuary, or a major route may transform the campaign without invalidating continuation where plausible.
playerFacingInfo: Known route status, confidence, access conditions, public warnings, and travel time
Major ship conditions, mission-pod configuration, shuttle availability, passenger load, evidence custody, and stores
Named favors, assets, access permissions, protected commitments, and public deadlines
playerAgency: Never write the player character's dialogue, thoughts, feelings, intentions, or decisions.
Do not assume acceptance of an order, moral premise, jurisdiction, bargain, relationship, or classification claim.
Prewritten approaches and Command Bearing prompts are examples only; freeform solutions remain valid.
Kellan's return, recovery, or command review may alter the chain of command only through explicit lawful process and committed state; it must not erase accumulated player agency.
